title: Hindi vs English Newspaper Readership Ratio
takeaway: Hindi newspapers outsell English ones 10:1 in UP; in Kerala, English dominates — media ecosystems are different countries.
policy: Language-specific misinformation travels through language-specific media; fact-checking must be multilingual.

title: Households with Television
takeaway: 90%+ of urban households have TV; in rural Bihar, under 50%.
policy: Television is still the primary information channel for rural India; digital-first strategies miss half the population.

title: Smartphone + Internet Access Digital Access P
takeaway: Smartphone + internet access exceeds 70% in metros but under 25% in rural NE states.
policy: Digital India assumes digital access; the access gap is a governance gap for DBT, CoWIN, and e-governance.

title: IFCN-Registered Fact-Check Organisations per
takeaway: IFCN-registered fact-check organisations are concentrated in metros — India's misinformation heartland has no fact-checkers.
policy: Fact-checking in Hindi and vernacular languages is critically underfunded relative to the scale of misinformation.

title: Womens Media Exposure Gap TV vs Print
takeaway: Women's media exposure gap (TV vs print) is widest in Bihar — women watch TV but don't read newspapers.
policy: Media format matters: health messaging through TV reaches more women; print campaigns miss the most vulnerable.

title: Pucca Housing Permanent Structure
takeaway: Over 90% of households in Kerala live in pucca structures; in Assam, under 40%.
policy: Housing quality predicts flood resilience, disease burden, and asset accumulation — it's a development indicator, not just shelter.

title: Average Monthly Per Capita Consumption Expend
takeaway: Monthly per-capita consumption expenditure in Delhi is 3x that of Chhattisgarh.
policy: Consumption inequality between states exceeds income inequality — the cost of living adjustment barely narrows the gap.

title: Gig Workers as % of Workforce
takeaway: Gig workers represent 3-8% of the workforce in metros but under 1% in most rural states.
policy: Gig work data is deeply uncertain; platform workers lack labour protections that even informal sector workers nominally have.

title: Agricultural Mechanisation Tractors per 1000
takeaway: Punjab has 100+ tractors per 1,000 hectares; eastern states have under 10.
policy: Agricultural mechanisation follows landholding size — marginal farmers can't afford tractors, deepening the productivity gap.

title: Fertiliser Consumption per Hectare
takeaway: Punjab and Haryana apply 200+ kg fertiliser per hectare; NE states apply under 30.
policy: Excess fertiliser use depletes soil health and pollutes water; under-use leaves yield potential on the table.

title: Crop Diversification Index
takeaway: Punjab and Haryana have the lowest crop diversification — rice-wheat monoculture dominates.
policy: Crop diversification is a climate adaptation and nutrition strategy; monoculture states are most vulnerable to price shocks.

title: Landless Agricultural Labourers
takeaway: In Bihar and Bengal, 40%+ of agricultural workers are landless labourers — they own nothing they cultivate.
policy: Landless labourers bear agricultural risk without owning assets; they are the most climate-vulnerable population.

title: Mudra Loans to SC ST per Lakh SC ST Populatio
takeaway: Mudra loans to SC/ST per lakh SC/ST population vary 5x — credit access is not equal.
policy: Mudra was designed for financial inclusion; if SC/ST communities can't access it equally, the scheme amplifies existing inequality.

title: Women SHG Membership per Lakh Women
takeaway: Women's SHG membership exceeds 10,000 per lakh women in southern states; in the North, under 2,000.
policy: SHGs are the most effective women's empowerment platform at scale; states with low SHG density miss this pathway entirely.

title: Domestic Violence FIR Filing Rate vs Experien
takeaway: In many states, the FIR filing rate for domestic violence is under 5% of women who report experiencing it.
policy: The gap between experience and FIR filing reveals how far the justice system is from actually protecting women.

title: Marital Rape Experience vs Reporting Gap
takeaway: Marital rape experience rates are high but reporting rates are near zero — it's not a crime under Indian law.
policy: India is one of 36 countries where marital rape is not criminalised; the data gap reflects the legal gap.

title: Acid Attack Cases per Crore Population
takeaway: Acid attack cases are concentrated in UP, West Bengal, and Delhi — but many go unreported.
policy: Acid attacks have declined since the Supreme Court restricted acid sales, but enforcement varies by state.

title: Missing Women Sen Methodology
takeaway: India has 63 million 'missing women' — the cumulative toll of sex-selective abortion, neglect, and violence.
policy: Amartya Sen's 'missing women' metric quantifies gender discrimination across the lifecycle, not just at birth.

title: Sex-Selective Abortion Proxy Unnatural Sex Ra
takeaway: States with sex ratios at birth below 900 girls per 1,000 boys show evidence of sex-selective practices.
policy: Pre-conception and pre-natal sex selection persists despite the PC-PNDT Act; enforcement is weakest in high-preference states.

title: Exclusive Breastfeeding in First 6 Months
takeaway: Exclusive breastfeeding in the first 6 months ranges from 75% in Chhattisgarh to under 40% in Nagaland.
policy: Exclusive breastfeeding is the single most cost-effective nutrition intervention — gaps reflect hospital practices and marketing pressure.

title: Children with Diarrhoea Receiving Zinc
takeaway: Only 30% of children with diarrhoea receive zinc treatment nationally — a cheap lifesaver underused.
policy: Zinc with ORS reduces diarrhoea duration by 25%; the gap is awareness and supply chain, not cost.

title: Adolescent Anaemia Girls 15-19
takeaway: Adolescent anaemia in girls (15-19) exceeds 60% in Jharkhand and Bihar.
policy: Anaemic adolescent girls become anaemic mothers who deliver anaemic babies — it's an intergenerational cycle.

title: Underweight Children Under 5
takeaway: Over 40% of under-5 children in Jharkhand and Bihar are underweight.
policy: Underweight children are more susceptible to infectious disease; nutrition and health outcomes are inseparable.

title: Households Using Improved Drinking Water Sour
takeaway: Improved drinking water source access exceeds 95% in Kerala but is under 60% in some NE states.
policy: Improved source ≠ safe water; even covered households face contamination, intermittent supply, and arsenic/fluoride.

title: Handwashing with Soap Households with Facilit
takeaway: Handwashing with soap facility exists in 80%+ of Kerala homes but under 30% in Odisha.
policy: COVID highlighted handwashing, but the infrastructure gap predates the pandemic — it's a structural WASH failure.

title: Open Defecation Still Practised
takeaway: Open defecation has dropped dramatically under SBM but persists at 10-30% in parts of Bihar and Jharkhand.
policy: Toilet construction ≠ toilet use; behaviour change lags behind infrastructure in the poorest states.

title: Households with No Sanitation Facility
takeaway: Households with no sanitation facility persist in eastern and NE India despite national campaigns.
policy: The last-mile sanitation challenge requires addressing water scarcity, land constraints, and cultural practices simultaneously.

title: Road Density km per 100 sq. km
takeaway: Kerala has 500+ km road per 100 sq km; Arunachal Pradesh has under 30.
policy: Road density determines market access, ambulance response time, and school connectivity — it's the infrastructure multiplier.

title: Railway Stations per 1000 sq. km
takeaway: Railway station density is highest in the Gangetic plain and coastal states; the NE is barely connected.
policy: Rail connectivity determines bulk goods movement, commuter mobility, and integration with the national economy.

title: Telecom Tower Density per 1000 sq. km
takeaway: Telecom tower density follows population density but lags in hilly and forested terrain.
policy: Telecom coverage gaps in NE and tribal areas mean digital governance doesn't reach the people who need it most.

title: Passport Holders as % of Population
takeaway: Passport holders exceed 15% of population in Kerala and Goa; in Bihar, under 2%.
policy: Passport holding is a proxy for international mobility, aspirational class size, and exposure to global opportunities.

title: Air Passengers per Lakh Population
takeaway: Air passengers per lakh population in Delhi/Goa is 50x that of Bihar.
policy: Aviation is a marker of economic integration; states without air connectivity are locked out of business networks.

title: Income Threshold Top 10% by State
takeaway: To be in the top 10% by income in Bihar, you need Rs 25,000/month; in Delhi, Rs 1.5 lakh.
policy: What counts as 'rich' varies 6x between states — national income brackets mask state-level reality.

title: Degree Attainment Gap Urban vs Rural
takeaway: The urban-rural gap in degree attainment exceeds 30 points in Bihar; in Kerala it's under 10.
policy: Education inequality within states (urban vs rural) can exceed inequality between states.

title: Conversational English Fluency
takeaway: Conversational English fluency is 30%+ in NE states and Kerala but under 5% in Hindi belt.
policy: English as a skill determines service-sector employability; the Hindi belt's English deficit limits its economic transition.

title: Households with Car Jeep or Van
takeaway: 20%+ of Delhi households own a car; in Bihar, under 2%.
policy: Car ownership maps purchasing power but also infrastructure load — cities with high ownership but poor roads gridlock.

title: Households with Computer or Laptop
takeaway: Computer/laptop ownership exceeds 30% in Delhi; in Bihar, under 5%.
policy: The digital device gap determines who can work remotely, access online education, and participate in the digital economy.

title: Distance to Nearest Bank Branch Rural km
takeaway: Average distance to the nearest bank branch in rural areas exceeds 10 km in many eastern states.
policy: Banking access is a physical problem — mobile banking helps but can't replace a branch for cash-dependent rural populations.

title: Maternal Undernutrition Women with BMI Below
takeaway: Maternal undernutrition (BMI below 18.5) exceeds 30% in Jharkhand and Bihar.
policy: Undernourished mothers deliver underweight babies who become stunted children — the cycle starts before conception.

title: Indebted Agricultural Households
takeaway: Over 50% of agricultural households in Andhra Pradesh and Telangana are indebted.
policy: Farm debt drives suicide risk; without income support or crop insurance, credit becomes a death trap.

title: Median Age by State
takeaway: Kerala's median age is 33; Bihar's is 20 — a 13-year gap that shapes everything from school demand to pension load.
policy: States at different demographic stages need fundamentally different fiscal priorities — one size fits none.

title: Sex Ratio Women per 1,000 Men
takeaway: Kerala has 1,084 women per 1,000 men; Haryana has 879 — a gap of 205 women reflecting decades of son preference.
policy: Sex ratio imbalances signal deep-rooted gender discrimination that no single policy can fix without cultural change.

title: Child Sex Ratio 0-6 Years Girls per 1,00
takeaway: Punjab and Haryana's child sex ratios (under 6) are among the worst globally — below 900 girls per 1,000 boys.
policy: Child sex ratios predict the marriage market crisis and trafficking patterns two decades ahead.

title: Transgender Population per Lakh
takeaway: Kerala and Tamil Nadu report the highest transgender population per lakh — states with stronger social movements.
policy: Visibility in data is the first step; most states still lack transgender welfare boards despite the 2014 NALSA ruling.

title: Decadal Population Growth Rate 2001-2011
takeaway: Bihar and Meghalaya grew at 25%+ in 2001-2011; Kerala and Goa at under 5% — India's demographic transition is staggered.
policy: Population growth drives demand for schools, hospitals, and jobs — fast-growing states need fiscal transfers, not blame.

title: Average Household Size
takeaway: Uttar Pradesh averages 6.1 persons per household; Goa averages 3.7 — reflecting joint vs nuclear family patterns.
policy: Household size determines housing demand, LPG cylinder allocation, and per-capita benefit calculations.

title: Dependency Ratio
takeaway: Bihar's dependency ratio exceeds 80; Goa's is under 40 — twice as many non-workers per worker in Bihar.
policy: High dependency ratios strain household budgets and limit savings, trapping states in low-investment cycles.

title: Share of Population Under 15 Years
takeaway: In Bihar, 40% of the population is under 15; in Kerala, it's 22% — two completely different Indias.
policy: Youth-heavy states need school infrastructure now; ageing states need geriatric care — the same budget formula fails both.

title: Share of Population Over 60 Years
takeaway: Kerala has 16% population over 60; Bihar has 7% — Kerala is ageing at European speeds with Indian incomes.
policy: Ageing states face a care economy crisis: who looks after the elderly when women enter the workforce?

title: Working Age Population 15-59 Share
takeaway: Southern and western states have 65%+ working-age populations — the demographic dividend window is already closing.
policy: The demographic dividend is not automatic; without jobs, a youth bulge becomes a source of unrest, not growth.

title: Projected State Population in 2036
takeaway: UP is projected to reach 260 million by 2036 — larger than Brazil — while Kerala barely grows.
policy: Delimitation based on 2036 projections will shift political power to high-fertility states that invested less in population control.

title: Net Migration Rate per 1,000 population
takeaway: Bihar and UP are net exporters of people; Maharashtra and Delhi are importers — migration maps economic opportunity.
policy: Internal migration subsidises destination states' economies while origin states lose their most productive workers.

title: Slum Dwellers as % of Urban Population
takeaway: Maharashtra's 40%+ urban population lives in slums; in many NE states it's near zero.
policy: Slum populations are invisible in GDP figures but visible in disease burden, fire deaths, and flood vulnerability.

title: Hindi as Mother Tongue
takeaway: Hindi is the mother tongue for 80%+ in UP/MP but under 1% in Tamil Nadu and Kerala — India's linguistic divide.
policy: Imposing Hindi-medium education or governance in non-Hindi states has measurably different cognitive costs for children.

title: Urban Population Share
takeaway: Tamil Nadu is 48% urban; Bihar is 11% — the gap in urbanisation is nearly five-fold.
policy: Urban states have denser service delivery but also denser pollution and housing crises; rural states face access deserts.

title: Vegetarian Population Share
takeaway: Rajasthan and Gujarat are 60%+ vegetarian; Bengal and Kerala are under 5% — India's food map defies the stereotype.
policy: Nutrition policy built on a vegetarian default fails in states where animal protein is the primary protein source.

title: Mobile Internet Penetration
takeaway: Delhi has 75%+ mobile internet penetration; Assam and Bihar are below 30%.
policy: Digital-first governance (DBT, CoWIN, e-Shram) systematically excludes states where digital access is lowest.

title: Hindu Temple Density per 100 sq. km
takeaway: Southern India has 3-5x the temple density of the Hindi heartland — challenging assumptions about where religiosity lives.
policy: Temple density maps cultural infrastructure, not just faith — they indicate tourism potential and land-use patterns.

title: Average Age of Women at First Marriage
takeaway: Women in Kerala marry at 23 on average; in Bihar, it's under 19 — a 4-year gap that changes life trajectories.
policy: Later marriage strongly correlates with women's education, workforce participation, and lower maternal mortality.

title: Graduate Degree Holders % of Adults 20
takeaway: In Delhi, 35%+ adults have graduate degrees; in Bihar, it's under 10%.
policy: Degree concentration in a few states creates brain-drain spirals — educated people leave, weakening the state further.

title: Beef and Buffalo Meat Consuming Househol
takeaway: Over 70% of households in Kerala and NE states consume beef or buffalo meat; in Rajasthan it's near zero.
policy: Beef bans have radically different economic impacts in states where cattle trade is a livelihood vs a cultural symbol.

title: Egg-Consuming Households
takeaway: Andhra Pradesh and Tamil Nadu are 90%+ egg-consuming; Rajasthan is under 20%.
policy: Egg supplementation in school meals — proven to reduce stunting — faces political resistance in states that need it most.

title: Rice as Primary Cereal % of total cere
takeaway: Rice dominates 90%+ of cereal consumption in the South and East; wheat dominates the North.
policy: PDS grain allocation still doesn't fully match local dietary preferences, causing nutrition and waste mismatches.

title: Fish-Consuming Households
takeaway: 80%+ households consume fish in Bengal, Odisha, and NE states; under 5% in Rajasthan and Haryana.
policy: Inland fisheries policy and cold chain infrastructure have massive returns in fish-eating states but zero relevance elsewhere.

title: Households Using Adequately Iodised Salt
takeaway: Only 60% of Indian households use adequately iodised salt — a preventable cause of cognitive impairment.
policy: Iodine deficiency disorders remain endemic in Himalayan and tribal belt states where iodised salt supply chains fail.

title: Women Consuming 5+ Food Groups Dietary D
takeaway: Only 25% of Indian women consume 5+ food groups daily — dietary diversity is a luxury in most states.
policy: Women eat last and least in joint families; dietary diversity programmes must address intra-household allocation, not just supply.

title: Linguistic Diversity Scheduled Languages
takeaway: Arunachal Pradesh has 30+ scheduled languages; Hindi-belt states have 1-2 — linguistic diversity is wildly uneven.
policy: Language policy in education (mother tongue vs link language) cannot be national — it must be state-specific.

title: Households Earning Above Rs 25000 Month
takeaway: Over 50% of households in Goa earn above Rs 25,000/month; in Bihar, it's under 10%.
policy: India's income inequality is not just between rich and poor people — it's between rich and poor states.

title: Multidimensional Poverty Index MPI
takeaway: Bihar's MPI is 0.35; Kerala's is 0.01 — a 35x gap in multidimensional poverty within one country.
policy: The MPI reveals that poverty is not just about income — it compounds across health, education, and living standards simultaneously.

title: Informal Workforce No Written Contract
takeaway: Over 70% of workers in Bihar and Jharkhand have no written contract — they are legally invisible.
policy: Informal workers have no access to social security, dispute resolution, or minimum wage enforcement.

title: Income Tax Filers as % of Population
takeaway: Only 2-3% of Bihar's population files income tax vs 15%+ in Delhi and Goa.
policy: The tax base is concentrated in a handful of states; fiscal federalism must account for this structural asymmetry.

title: MGNREGA Average Days of Work per Household
takeaway: Tamil Nadu provides 50+ days of MGNREGA work per household; Bihar provides under 30.
policy: MGNREGA is demand-driven in theory, but supply-constrained in practice — poorer states deliver fewer days.

title: Night-time Light Radiance Economic Activity P
takeaway: Night-time light intensity in Mumbai is 100x that of rural Bihar — satellite data confirms the development gap.
policy: Night-light data is a bias-free proxy for economic activity, useful where GDP estimates are contested.

title: GSDP Per Capita Rs Lakh
takeaway: Goa's GSDP per capita is Rs 5.5 lakh; Bihar's is Rs 0.5 lakh — an 11x gap.
policy: Per-capita income gaps between Indian states now exceed gaps between many countries.

title: Court Case Backlog per Judge
takeaway: A judge in UP has 3,000+ pending cases; in Sikkim, under 500 — justice delivery collapses under load.
policy: Court backlog is not just a legal problem — it delays land disputes, business contracts, and criminal justice.

title: Bonded Labour Identified and Released cumulat
takeaway: Over 90% of identified bonded labourers have been found in just 5 states — the practice persists despite laws.
policy: Bonded labour rehabilitation requires proactive identification; states that don't look, don't find.

title: Human Trafficking Cases per 10 lakh populatio
takeaway: Trafficking cases cluster in Assam, WB, and Jharkhand — the corridor follows poverty and migration routes.
policy: Anti-trafficking policy must focus on source states, not just destination cities.

title: LPG as Primary Cooking Fuel
takeaway: 98% of Delhi households use LPG; in Bihar, it's under 40% despite Ujjwala — affordability kills adoption.
policy: LPG connections ≠ LPG use; refill costs push poor households back to biomass within months of getting a connection.

title: Households with Piped Water Tap Connection
takeaway: Goa and Himachal have 90%+ piped water; in Jharkhand and Assam, it's under 20%.
policy: JJM tap connections don't guarantee water quality or continuous supply — functionality matters more than coverage.

title: Average Daily Electricity Supply Rural Hours
takeaway: Rural Punjab gets 20+ hours of electricity daily; rural Bihar gets under 12.
policy: Electricity supply determines irrigation, cold storage, and digital access — it's a binding constraint on rural GDP.

title: Bank Branches per Lakh Population
takeaway: Karnataka has 16+ bank branches per lakh; NE states have under 8.
policy: Financial inclusion metrics look good nationally, but banking density varies 2-3x between states.

title: Post Offices per Lakh Population
takeaway: India's post office network is denser than its bank network, especially in rural areas — a legacy asset.
policy: Post offices can deliver last-mile financial services, pensions, and insurance where banks won't open branches.

title: Pucca Permanent Structure Housing
takeaway: 90%+ of Kerala households live in permanent (pucca) structures; in Assam and Bihar, it's under 50%.
policy: Housing quality directly predicts flood resilience, disease burden, and household wealth accumulation.

title: Children Under 5 Who Are Stunted
takeaway: 38% of under-5 children in Bihar are stunted; in Kerala it's 12% — being born in the wrong state costs you inches.
policy: Stunting is irreversible after age 2; targeted nutrition in the first 1,000 days must be a state-level emergency.

title: Anaemia in Women 15-49
takeaway: Over 65% of women in Jharkhand and Bihar are anaemic vs 33% in Manipur.
policy: Anaemia saps productivity, worsens maternal mortality, and passes disadvantage to the next generation.

title: Households with Functional Toilet
takeaway: 97% of Kerala households have a functional toilet; in Bihar it was under 40% until recently.
policy: Sanitation coverage improved dramatically under SBM, but usage lags behind construction in many states.

title: Institutional Delivery
takeaway: 99% of deliveries in Kerala and Tamil Nadu are institutional; in Nagaland it's under 50%.
policy: Institutional delivery is the single strongest predictor of neonatal survival — distance to facility is the bottleneck.

title: Full Immunisation Coverage 12-23 months
takeaway: Full immunisation coverage is 90%+ in Odisha but under 60% in Nagaland — a 30-point gap.
policy: Immunisation gaps track health system reach; states with low coverage also miss other primary healthcare.

title: Maternal Mortality Ratio
takeaway: Assam's MMR is 215 per 100,000 live births; Kerala's is 19 — a 11x gap in maternal death risk.
policy: Maternal mortality is the starkest measure of health system failure — most of these deaths are preventable.

title: Annual Mean PM2.5 Concentration
takeaway: Delhi's PM2.5 averages 100+ µg/m³ — 20x the WHO guideline; southern states are 3-4x lower.
policy: Air pollution kills more Indians than tobacco; the health burden is concentrated in the Indo-Gangetic plain.

title: Government Health Expenditure per Capita
takeaway: Kerala spends Rs 2,500+ per capita on public health; Bihar spends under Rs 500.
policy: Low public health spending forces the poor into private care, triggering catastrophic out-of-pocket expenditure.

title: Under-5 Mortality Rate
takeaway: Under-5 mortality in Madhya Pradesh is 65 per 1,000; in Kerala it's 4 — a 16x gap.
policy: A child's survival probability should not depend on which state they are born in.

title: Neonatal Mortality Rate First 28 Days
takeaway: Neonatal mortality (first 28 days) accounts for 60%+ of infant deaths — most in the first 48 hours.
policy: Neonatal deaths cluster around birth; facility-based newborn care units in district hospitals save the most lives per rupee.

title: C-Section Delivery Rate
takeaway: Telangana's C-section rate is 57%; Nagaland's is 6% — both are problematic, but for opposite reasons.
policy: High C-section rates signal over-medicalisation in private hospitals; low rates signal surgical access failures.

title: Low Birth Weight Babies
takeaway: 20%+ of babies in UP and MP are born underweight; in Mizoram it's under 5%.
policy: Low birth weight predicts lifelong health disadvantage — it begins before birth, with maternal nutrition.

title: Diarrhoea Treatment with ORS
takeaway: ORS use during childhood diarrhoea ranges from 75% in Odisha to under 30% in some NE states.
policy: ORS is the simplest, cheapest lifesaver in child health — gaps reflect awareness and supply chain failures.

title: Children with ARI Symptoms Taken to Health Fa
takeaway: In Bihar, under 60% of children with ARI symptoms are taken to a health facility.
policy: Acute respiratory infections kill more children than malaria; care-seeking behaviour depends on facility proximity and trust.

title: Doctors per Lakh Population
takeaway: Delhi has 14+ doctors per lakh population; Bihar has under 3 — India's health workforce follows the money.
policy: Doctors concentrate in urban areas and rich states; mandatory rural posting or telemedicine are the only solutions.

title: Hospital Beds per Lakh Population
takeaway: Kerala has 5+ hospital beds per 1,000; Bihar has under 1 — below the WHO minimum.
policy: Hospital bed density determines surge capacity during epidemics and disaster response readiness.

title: PM-JAY Ayushman Bharat Hospital Admissions pe
takeaway: Ayushman Bharat hospital admissions per lakh vary 10x between states — utilisation ≠ coverage.
policy: PM-JAY provides insurance, but without hospitals, the card is unusable — supply-side gaps limit demand-side schemes.

title: Out-of-Pocket Health Expenditure
takeaway: Out-of-pocket health spending exceeds 70% of total health expenditure in UP and Bihar.
policy: Catastrophic health expenditure pushes 6 crore Indians below the poverty line every year.

title: Life Expectancy at Birth
takeaway: Life expectancy in Kerala is 77 years; in Chhattisgarh it's 64 — a 13-year gap within one country.
policy: The 13-year life expectancy gap between Indian states exceeds the gap between some developed and developing nations.

title: TB Incidence per Lakh Population
takeaway: TB incidence in UP and Bihar exceeds 300 per lakh; in Kerala it's under 60.
policy: India has the world's largest TB burden; notification gaps mean many cases are treated in private sector without reporting.

title: HIV Prevalence Among Adults 15-49
takeaway: HIV prevalence is concentrated in Mizoram, Nagaland, and Manipur (NE) and southern states.
policy: HIV responses must be state-tailored: NE states face injection-driven epidemics vs sexually transmitted in the South.

title: Girls Married Before 18
takeaway: 40% of women in Bihar were married before 18; in Goa and Kerala it's under 5%.
policy: Child marriage is the single strongest predictor of girls dropping out, early pregnancy, and domestic violence.

title: Sex Ratio at Birth Girls per 1000 Boys
takeaway: Haryana and Punjab's sex ratio at birth (under 900 girls per 1,000 boys) signals active sex selection.
policy: Pre-natal sex determination bans exist but enforcement is weak where son preference is strongest.

title: Female Labour Force Participation Rate
takeaway: Female labour force participation in Himachal is 58%; in Bihar it's under 5% — a 10x gap.
policy: India's female LFPR varies more between states than between many countries — there is no single 'Indian' story.

title: Women Who Make Financial Decisions Alone or J
takeaway: In southern states, 80%+ women participate in household financial decisions; in Bihar, under 40%.
policy: Women's financial decision-making is a gateway indicator — it predicts savings, child nutrition, and education investment.

title: Women Who Justify Wife-Beating in At Least On
takeaway: In Bihar, 40%+ women believe a husband is justified in beating his wife; in Manipur, under 10%.
policy: When women themselves normalise violence, the problem is not just legal — it's about generations of internalised norms.

title: Women with No Exposure to Any Media
takeaway: Over 50% of women in Bihar and Jharkhand have zero media exposure — no TV, radio, or newspapers.
policy: Media-dark women are unreachable by awareness campaigns, behaviour change communication, and even emergency warnings.

title: Women with Bank Account Used Independently
takeaway: Over 80% of women in southern states have and use a bank account independently; in Bihar under 30%.
policy: Financial inclusion for women is not about account opening (Jan Dhan) — it's about independent usage and control.

title: Domestic Violence Women Reporting Physical Vi
takeaway: 33% of Indian women have experienced physical violence from a spouse — but rates vary from 10% to 45% by state.
policy: Domestic violence is not uniformly distributed; state-level variation reveals where enforcement and shelters are most needed.

title: Dowry Deaths Rate per Lakh Women
takeaway: Uttar Pradesh reports the highest absolute number of dowry deaths — over 2,000 annually.
policy: Dowry deaths are just the visible tip; dowry harassment and extortion are vastly underreported across all states.

title: Honour Killings Reported Cases
takeaway: Honour killing data is deeply uncertain — some states report zero, which likely reflects non-reporting, not absence.
policy: Honour killings often go unreported or are classified as suicides/accidents; this map shows reported cases, not reality.

title: Witch-Hunting Deaths
takeaway: Jharkhand, Odisha, and Assam report the most witch-hunting deaths — targeting poor, widowed, tribal women.
policy: Witch-hunting persists where land disputes intersect with superstition and weak police presence.

title: Women with 10+ Years of Schooling
takeaway: 70%+ of women in Kerala and Goa have 10+ years of schooling; in Bihar, under 25%.
policy: Women's education is the single most powerful lever for reducing fertility, child mortality, and domestic violence.

title: Women MLAs as % of Total MLAs
takeaway: Women MLAs constitute over 15% in Rajasthan and Haryana (post-reservation) but under 5% in many states.
policy: Women's political representation is still driven by reservation mandates, not organic party selection.

title: Teen Pregnancy Rate Women 15-19 Who Are Mothe
takeaway: In Tripura and Assam, 10%+ of women aged 15-19 are already mothers; in Goa, under 1%.
policy: Teenage pregnancy ends education, increases maternal risk, and perpetuates intergenerational poverty.

title: Creches per Lakh Working Women
takeaway: Creche availability per working women varies enormously — childcare is India's missing infrastructure.
policy: Without affordable childcare, women either don't enter the workforce or leave when they have children.

title: Female Domestic Workers as % of All Workers
takeaway: Female domestic workers are concentrated in metros and southern states — an invisible workforce.
policy: Domestic workers lack minimum wage protection, social security, and safe working conditions in most states.

title: SC + ST Population Share
takeaway: SC+ST populations exceed 50% in Mizoram and Meghalaya; in Goa and Delhi, under 10%.
policy: Reservation policy has uniform percentages nationally, but caste demographics vary enormously by state.

title: SC Households Owning Agricultural Land
takeaway: In Punjab, over 30% of SC households own agricultural land; in Bihar, under 5%.
policy: Land ownership is the strongest predictor of Dalit economic mobility — landless SC families stay trapped.

title: SC vs General Immunisation Coverage Gap
takeaway: The immunisation gap between SC and general category children exceeds 15 points in several states.
policy: Caste-based health disparities persist even in states with high overall coverage — averages mask exclusion.

title: Inter-Caste Marriages
takeaway: Inter-caste marriage rates remain below 10% nationally — highest in Kerala, lowest in Rajasthan.
policy: Inter-caste marriage is the most intimate measure of social integration; legislation incentivises but hasn't normalised it.

title: Crimes Against SCs per Lakh SC Population
takeaway: Rajasthan and UP report the highest crimes against SCs — but high reporting may also indicate better police access.
policy: NCRB crime data reflects reporting rates, not just incidence; states with low numbers may be suppressing complaints.

title: Manual Scavenging States Where It Persists
takeaway: Manual scavenging persists in UP, MP, Rajasthan, and Maharashtra despite being illegal since 1993.
policy: Three laws ban manual scavenging, but without sewer network investment and mechanisation, the practice continues.

title: SC Representation in IAS vs Population Share
takeaway: SC representation in IAS is 15% against a 16.6% population share — close, but promotion gaps widen at senior levels.
policy: Entry-level representation has improved; the ceiling is at joint secretary and above, where SC officers are underrepresented.

title: Death Registration Completeness
takeaway: Death registration completeness exceeds 95% in Kerala but is under 50% in Bihar and Jharkhand.
policy: Incomplete death registration means India doesn't know how many people die or from what — the basis of health planning.

title: Birth Registration Children Under 5
takeaway: Birth registration for children under 5 exceeds 95% in southern states but is under 60% in Assam and Nagaland.
policy: Unregistered children have no legal identity — they can't access school admission, welfare schemes, or age-proof documentation.

title: Non-Hindu Population Share
takeaway: In Mizoram, Meghalaya, and Nagaland, over 85% of the population is non-Hindu — India's religious map is not monolithic.
policy: Religious composition determines school systems, family law, dietary norms, and festival holidays at the state level.

title: Christian Population Share
takeaway: Christians form 87% of Nagaland's population but only 2.3% nationally — extreme geographic concentration.
policy: Christian minority institutions (schools, hospitals) are the primary service providers in NE states — not a marginal presence.

title: Muslim Multidimensional Poverty Rate
takeaway: Muslim multidimensional poverty rates exceed 50% in UP and Bihar vs under 15% in Kerala.
policy: Muslim poverty varies more between states than between communities — state-level development matters more than community identity.

title: Muslim Total Fertility Rate
takeaway: Muslim TFR has converged toward Hindu TFR in southern states but remains 0.5-1.0 higher in UP and Bihar.
policy: Fertility is driven by education and income, not theology — Muslim TFR in Kerala equals Hindu TFR in UP.

title: Communal Riots by State 2019-2024
takeaway: Communal riots are concentrated in UP, Rajasthan, Gujarat, and MP — not evenly distributed.
policy: Riot patterns correlate with electoral cycles and police deployment gaps, not just communal demographics.

title: Buddhist Population Share
takeaway: Maharashtra has the largest Buddhist population (6%), driven by Dr. Ambedkar's conversion movement.
policy: Neo-Buddhism maps directly onto SC political mobilisation — religion and caste identity intersect here.

title: Tribal   Indigenous Religion Share
takeaway: Tribal/indigenous religion adherents are concentrated in Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, and NE states.
policy: Indigenous religions are distinct from Hinduism — census classification as 'Hindu' erases distinct identities and land rights.

title: Religion Not Stated
takeaway: In Manipur and Meghalaya, 'religion not stated' is above average — reflecting contested identities.
policy: The 'not stated' category can mask religious fluidity, conversion anxieties, or census refusal.

title: People Who Say Religion Is Very Important in
takeaway: 90%+ of Indians say religion is 'very important' in their lives — but the figure varies 20+ points between states.
policy: High religiosity states prioritise temple/mosque construction; lower-religiosity states invest more in secular public goods.

title: Hindu Denominational Diversity Vaishnavism vs
takeaway: Hindu denominational diversity (Vaishnavism vs Shaivism vs other) varies sharply — South is more Shaiva, North more Vaishnava.
policy: Denominational diversity within Hinduism is invisible in policy but drives food culture, festival calendars, and temple economics.

title: ASER Class 5 Children Who Cannot Read Class 2
takeaway: In Bihar, 60%+ of Class 5 students cannot read a Class 2 text — they are in school but not learning.
policy: Enrolment without learning is India's real education crisis; the ASER data shows schooling ≠ education.

title: English Speakers % Who Speak English as 1st
takeaway: In Nagaland and Meghalaya, 30%+ speak English as their first or second language; in Bihar, under 1%.
policy: English proficiency maps onto economic opportunity in the service sector — NE states have an underrecognised advantage.

title: Graduate Degree Holders % of Adults 20+
takeaway: Delhi has 35%+ graduate degree holders among adults; Bihar has under 8%.
policy: Degree concentration creates self-reinforcing cycles — graduates cluster where jobs are, and jobs follow graduates.

title: Female Literacy Rate
takeaway: Female literacy in Kerala is 96%; in Rajasthan it's 57% — a 39-point gap.
policy: Female literacy is the master variable — it predicts fertility, child survival, nutrition, and women's agency better than income.

title: Teacher Vacancy Rate in Government Schools
takeaway: Teacher vacancy rates in government schools exceed 40% in UP and Bihar — classrooms without teachers.
policy: Teacher vacancies are concentrated where they hurt most; vacant positions in poor states widen the learning gap.

title: Private School Enrolment Share Classes 1-12
takeaway: In Rajasthan and UP, 50%+ of enrolled students are in private schools; in the NE, under 15%.
policy: The private school boom reflects government school quality collapse — not just parental preference.

title: Internet Used for Information or Knowledge
takeaway: Internet used for information/knowledge varies 3x between Delhi and Bihar.
policy: Digital learning access during COVID was highly unequal — the digital divide is an education divide.

title: Out-of-School Children 6-14 Years
takeaway: Out-of-school children (6-14) cluster in UP, Bihar, and Rajasthan — driven by poverty, child labour, and distance.
policy: The last-mile children are hardest to reach — migrant families, disabled children, and girls in rural areas.

title: Pupil-Teacher Ratio in Government Primary Sch
takeaway: Pupil-teacher ratio in government primary schools exceeds 40:1 in Bihar; in Kerala it's under 25:1.
policy: High PTR means less individual attention, weaker discipline, and worse learning outcomes — a direct quality indicator.

title: Mid-Day Meal Coverage vs Enrolment
takeaway: Mid-day meal coverage vs enrolment shows leakage in several states — meals allocated but not reaching children.
policy: The mid-day meal scheme is India's largest nutrition programme; leakage means both wasted funds and hungry children.

title: Disability Prevalence
takeaway: Disability prevalence varies 2-3x between states — likely reflecting measurement differences, not actual rates.
policy: States that screen better find more disability; under-identification means under-provision of support services.

title: Annual Rainfall
takeaway: Meghalaya receives 11,000+ mm of annual rainfall; Rajasthan gets under 400 — a 28x difference.
policy: Water availability is the foundational resource constraint — irrigation, drinking water, and industry all depend on it.

title: Net Irrigated Area as % of Net Sown Area
takeaway: Punjab irrigates 98% of its net sown area; Jharkhand irrigates under 10%.
policy: Rainfed agriculture is a climate gamble; unirrigated states are most vulnerable to monsoon failure and climate change.

title: Mean June Temperature
takeaway: Mean June temperature in Rajasthan and Vidarbha exceeds 40°C; in the NE, it's under 25°C.
policy: Heat stress reduces labour productivity by 15-20% — outdoor workers in hot states lose both income and health.

title: Extreme Heat Days Above 40C per Year
takeaway: Parts of Rajasthan and Andhra Pradesh experience 40+ days above 40°C annually — and rising.
policy: Extreme heat is India's most underestimated climate risk; heatwave action plans exist in only a few cities.

title: Critically Polluted River Stretches
takeaway: UP and Maharashtra have the most critically polluted river stretches — industrial and sewage contamination.
policy: River pollution is a governance failure — STPs exist in plans but operate at fraction of capacity.

title: Water-Intensive Crops vs Millet Area Ratio
takeaway: Punjab and Haryana grow 10x more water-intensive crops (rice, sugarcane) per unit of millet area.
policy: Growing rice in Punjab depletes groundwater at unsustainable rates; millet revival is a water security strategy.

title: Forest Cover Change 2001-2023
takeaway: Northeast states gained forest cover; central Indian states lost it — mining and urbanisation are the drivers.
policy: India's aggregate forest cover number hides redistribution — plantation gains in one state mask old-growth loss in another.

title: Municipal Solid Waste Per Capita
takeaway: Delhi generates 3x the per-capita municipal solid waste of rural India — urban consumption drives waste.
policy: Waste management infrastructure hasn't kept pace with urbanisation; most Indian cities landfill 90%+ of waste.

title: Flood-Prone Districts
takeaway: Assam, Bihar, and UP have the most flood-prone districts — the Brahmaputra and Gangetic floodplain.
policy: Flood-prone states need permanent flood management infrastructure, not just emergency relief every monsoon.

title: Groundwater Over-Exploited Blocks
takeaway: Punjab and Haryana have the most over-exploited groundwater blocks — the Green Revolution's hidden cost.
policy: Groundwater is being mined, not harvested; free electricity for pumping accelerates the crisis.

title: PM Fasal Bima Crop Insurance Coverage
takeaway: Crop insurance (PMFBY) coverage varies 5x between states — farmer awareness and insurer presence matter.
policy: Crop insurance is the primary climate adaptation tool for small farmers; uneven coverage leaves the most vulnerable exposed.

title: Renewable Energy Capacity per Capita
takeaway: Rajasthan and Gujarat lead in renewable energy capacity per capita; NE states trail significantly.
policy: Renewable energy potential maps onto geography (solar in Rajasthan, wind in Gujarat) but policy drives deployment.

title: Clean Cooking Fuel as Primary Fuel
takeaway: 95%+ of Kerala households use clean cooking fuel; in Bihar, over 50% still burn biomass.
policy: Indoor air pollution from biomass cooking kills more women and children than outdoor air pollution in rural India.

title: Voter Turnout 2024 Lok Sabha
takeaway: Lakshadweep hit 84% turnout in 2024; Bihar was at 57% — a 27-point gap in democratic participation.
policy: Low turnout concentrates power among motivated voters; improving turnout in lagging states requires better booth access.

title: Lok Sabha Seats Held by Same Party Since 1984
takeaway: Some Lok Sabha seats have been held by the same party for 40 years — political competition is not uniform.
policy: Dominant-party constituencies receive less opposition scrutiny and often underperform on development indicators.

title: Elected MPs with Criminal Cases
takeaway: In UP and Bihar, 40%+ of elected MPs have criminal cases — the highest in India.
policy: Criminalisation of politics deters honest candidates and normalises impunity; disclosure hasn't reduced winnability.

title: Police per Lakh Population
takeaway: Delhi has 250+ police per lakh; Bihar has under 80 — India's police are thinnest where crime is densest.
policy: Police understaffing means slower response, weaker investigation, and lower conviction rates — a justice deficit.

title: Internet Shutdowns Cumulative 2012-2024
takeaway: J&K alone accounts for 60%+ of cumulative internet shutdowns since 2012.
policy: Internet shutdowns cost India $4.8 billion in 2020; they suppress free expression and throttle digital commerce.

title: RTI Applications per Lakh Population
takeaway: Kerala and Maharashtra file the most RTI applications per capita; UP and Bihar file the fewest.
policy: RTI usage maps civic awareness; states where citizens demand transparency tend to have better governance outcomes.

title: NCRB Crimes Against Women Rate
takeaway: Assam and UP report the highest rates of crimes against women — but reporting ≠ incidence.
policy: NCRB data on crimes against women conflates registration culture with actual violence; safer-seeming states may suppress reports.

title: Youth 18-25 vs Elderly 65+ Voter Turnout Gap
takeaway: In several states, elderly voters (65+) turn out at higher rates than youth (18-25).
policy: Youth disengagement from elections is a democratic warning — if young people don't vote, policy skews toward older interests.

title: Old Age Pension Coverage % of Eligible
takeaway: Old age pension coverage reaches 80%+ of eligible in some states but under 30% in others.
policy: Pension coverage depends on state implementation capacity; central funding alone doesn't guarantee last-mile delivery.

title: Disability Pension Coverage
takeaway: Disability pension coverage varies even more sharply than old age pensions — disabled citizens are doubly excluded.
policy: Disability identification, certification, and pension delivery all fail in low-capacity states.

title: Newspaper Readership per 1000 Population
takeaway: Newspaper readership per 1,000 exceeds 300 in Kerala; in several NE states it's under 50.
policy: Print media density tracks civic participation; media deserts often coincide with governance deserts.

title: Ever Voted as % of Eligible Adults
takeaway: In Kerala, 90%+ of eligible adults have ever voted in any election; in Bihar, it's under 70%.
policy: Habitual non-voting is concentrated among the poorest and least educated — the very people who need policy most.

title: MSME Density per Lakh Population
takeaway: Tamil Nadu and Gujarat have 3x the MSME density of Bihar — small enterprise is geographically concentrated.
policy: MSME clusters create local employment multipliers; fostering them in lagging states requires infrastructure + credit, not just policy.

title: Manufacturing GVA Share of GSDP
takeaway: Tamil Nadu and Gujarat get 20%+ of GSDP from manufacturing; NE states get under 5%.
policy: Manufacturing is India's job-creation engine, but it concentrates in states with power, ports, and skills — leaving others behind.

title: Agriculture Share of GSDP
takeaway: Agriculture contributes 30%+ to GSDP in MP and Chhattisgarh but under 5% in Delhi and Goa.
policy: States dependent on agriculture face income volatility, climate risk, and terms-of-trade decline.

title: Marginal Farmers Holdings Under 1 Hectare
takeaway: In Bihar and Bengal, 80%+ of farm holdings are under 1 hectare — marginal farmers with marginal income.
policy: Marginal farmers cannot mechanise, diversify, or access formal credit — farm policy designed for Punjab doesn't work here.

title: Services Share of GSDP
takeaway: Services contribute 60%+ of GSDP in Delhi, Maharashtra, and Karnataka — the new growth engine.
policy: Service-sector growth benefits educated workers in metros; manufacturing must absorb the less-skilled majority.

title: Child Labour Working Children 5-14
takeaway: Child labour (5-14) remains significant in UP, Bihar, and Rajasthan despite legal prohibition.
policy: Child labour data is methodologically contested post-2011; proxy indicators suggest the problem persists in informal sectors.

title: Women in Non-Agricultural Employment
takeaway: Women in non-agricultural employment are concentrated in southern and western states.
policy: Non-agricultural jobs for women signal economic transformation; states where women are stuck in agriculture are falling behind.

title: Remittance-Dependent States
takeaway: Bihar and UP are the most remittance-dependent states — migrant earnings prop up rural consumption.
policy: Remittance dependence signals local job failure; when destination cities slow down, origin states face consumption crashes.

title: DPIIT-Recognised Startups per Lakh Population
takeaway: Karnataka has 50x the startup density of Bihar — innovation ecosystems are radically unequal.
policy: Startup concentration follows venture capital, talent, and internet — a self-reinforcing cycle hard for lagging states to break.

title: PM-JAY Hospital Admissions per Lakh Beneficia
takeaway: PM-JAY utilisation per lakh beneficiaries varies 10x — having insurance ≠ being able to use it.
policy: Supply-side gaps (hospitals, empanelled facilities) determine whether demand-side schemes reach people.

title: MGNREGA Women as % of Total Person-Days
takeaway: Women constitute 60%+ of MGNREGA person-days in Tamil Nadu and Kerala but under 20% in UP.
policy: MGNREGA is India's largest women's employment programme where women participate; enabling women's access elsewhere is a policy win.

title: PDS Ration Card Coverage vs Actual Offtake
takeaway: PDS coverage vs actual grain offtake shows leakage exceeding 40% in some states.
policy: The gap between ration cards issued and grain actually distributed reveals both diversion and exclusion errors.

title: Anganwadi Centres per 1000 Children 0-6
takeaway: Anganwadi centre density per 1,000 children varies 3x between states.
policy: Anganwadis are India's primary early childhood system; density gaps translate directly into nutrition and cognitive gaps.

title: Tobacco Use Men
takeaway: Over 50% of men in Mizoram and Tripura use tobacco; in Goa, under 15%.
policy: Tobacco is India's largest preventable killer; taxation and cessation programmes need state-specific strategies.

title: Tobacco Use Women
takeaway: Women's tobacco use is highest in the NE (40%+) and lowest in Punjab and Haryana (under 2%).
policy: Smokeless tobacco among women (gudakhu, khaini) is normalised in NE states — different products need different interventions.

title: Alcohol Consumption Men
takeaway: Alcohol consumption among men exceeds 50% in Arunachal Pradesh; several states are near zero due to prohibition.
policy: Prohibition states show lower consumption but higher illicit liquor deaths — the policy creates its own risks.

title: Hypertension Women
takeaway: Hypertension in women exceeds 30% in Kerala and Punjab — the NCD transition is well advanced in richer states.
policy: As states get richer, their disease burden shifts from infectious to chronic — health systems must adapt.

title: Hypertension Men
takeaway: Hypertension in men follows a similar pattern — higher in southern and western states.
policy: Male hypertension combined with lower health-seeking behaviour means undiagnosed cardiovascular risk is highest among men.

title: Diabetes Prevalence Adults
takeaway: Diabetes prevalence ranges from 15%+ in Kerala/Goa to under 5% in Chhattisgarh.
policy: India's diabetes epidemic is concentrated in states undergoing nutrition transition — urban diets + sedentary lifestyles.

title: Female Sterilisation as % of Modern Contrac
takeaway: Female sterilisation accounts for 70%+ of modern contraception in Andhra Pradesh and Madhya Pradesh.
policy: When sterilisation dominates, women lose the option of spacing births — it's a one-time choice, not ongoing autonomy.

title: Condom Use by Currently Married Couples
takeaway: Condom use by married couples is under 10% in most states — male methods remain marginalised.
policy: Condom promotion has failed nationally; the contraceptive burden remains almost entirely on women.

title: IUD   Copper-T Use Among Married Women
takeaway: IUD/Copper-T use varies from 2% to 12% — it's high in UP (government push) and low in southern states.
policy: IUD use reflects provider preference more than women's choice — insertion targets drive clinical behaviour.

title: Pill Usage Among Married Women
takeaway: Pill use is highest in Bengal and Assam (15%+), minimal elsewhere — cultural adoption patterns drive method mix.
policy: Contraceptive method mix should reflect women's preferences, not supply-side biases of the health system.

title: Unmet Need for Contraception
takeaway: Unmet need for contraception exceeds 15% in Bihar and Jharkhand — women want to prevent pregnancy but can't.
policy: Unmet need means women lack access, information, or agency — not that they want more children.

title: Vasectomy Rate Among Married Men
takeaway: Vasectomy rates are below 1% nationally — India's family planning programme never invested in male methods.
policy: The near-zero vasectomy rate is a policy failure, not a biological inevitability — it reflects gendered programme design.

title: Men Who Believe Family Planning Is Women's Re
takeaway: In UP and Rajasthan, 40%+ men believe family planning is women's responsibility.
policy: Men's attitudes toward contraception directly determine women's access; male engagement programmes are critically underfunded.

title: Overweight and Obese Women
takeaway: Overweight/obese women exceed 40% in Delhi, Punjab, and Kerala — the nutrition transition is real.
policy: Obesity and undernutrition coexist in the same states — the 'double burden' requires integrated nutrition policy.

title: Overweight and Obese Men
takeaway: Male obesity follows similar patterns — higher in urban, wealthy, southern states.
policy: Male overweight is rising faster than female in many states; lifestyle disease prevention must target men too.

title: Psychiatrist Density per Lakh Population
takeaway: India has 0.3 psychiatrists per lakh population — and 80% of them are in 5 states.
policy: Mental health is India's most under-resourced health domain; most districts have zero psychiatric professionals.

title: HIV Prevalence Among Adults 15-49
takeaway: HIV prevalence among adults is concentrated in NE states (injection-driven) and southern states (sexually transmitted).
policy: India's HIV epidemic has two distinct geographies requiring two distinct prevention strategies.
